questions.question: Explain the concept of file sharing semantics in operating systems. What are the different types of semantics?
keyPoints: File sharing semantics define behavior when multiple processes access same file
UNIX semantics: changes immediately visible to all processes with file open
Session semantics: changes visible only after file is closed and reopened
Immutable semantics: once opened, file cannot be modified
Append semantics: only append operations allowed

questions.question: Differentiate between exclusive (write) locks and shared (read) locks in file systems.
keyPoints: Exclusive lock: only one process can access file for writing; blocks all other access
Shared lock: multiple processes can read simultaneously; blocks exclusive locks
Purpose: prevent data inconsistency during concurrent access
Release: both locks must be released after use

questions.question: What is a deadlock in the context of file sharing? Explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock.
keyPoints: Deadlock: circular wait where processes wait for locks held by each other
Mutual Exclusion: locks are non-sharable resources
Hold and Wait: processes hold locks while waiting for additional locks
No Preemption: locks cannot be forcibly taken away
Circular Wait: circular chain of processes each waiting for lock held by next

questions.question: Describe the readers-writers problem. How does writer priority help prevent writer starvation?
keyPoints: Readers-writers: multiple readers allowed simultaneously, writers need exclusive access
Writer starvation: readers keep arriving, writer never gets access
Writer priority solution: when writer is waiting, block new readers from starting
This ensures queued writer gets access once current readers finish
Fairness: balances concurrency with preventing indefinite waiting

questions.question: Explain copy-on-write (COW) technique in file sharing. How does it improve efficiency?
keyPoints: COW: multiple processes share same data pages initially
No immediate copying of entire file on fork() or shared access
Copy created only when a process modifies data
Original data preserved for other processes
Reduces memory overhead and improves performance for read-heavy workloads

questions.question: Differentiate between mandatory locking and advisory locking in UNIX systems.
keyPoints: Advisory: OS provides lock mechanisms but doesn't enforce; processes must voluntarily check
Mandatory: OS enforces locks at kernel level; blocks unauthorized access
Mandatory requires special file permission (setgid + setgid bit)
Advisory is default in most UNIX systems
Mandatory can cause unexpected blocking of uncooperative processes

questions.question: What are file leases in distributed file systems? How do they improve consistency?
keyPoints: Lease: time-limited lock granted to client in distributed systems
Client gets exclusive access for specified duration (e.g., 30-60 seconds)
Lease automatically expires after timeout
Server can grant file to another client after expiration
Improves consistency by preventing indefinite locks in distributed environment

questions.question: Consider two processes P1 and P2 both trying to access a shared file. Process P1 acquires a shared lock and later wants to upgrade it to an exclusive lock. Process P2 also wants to acquire a shared lock. Analyze potential deadlock scenarios and suggest solutions to prevent deadlock during lock upgrade.
keyPoints: Deadlock Scenario: P1 has shared lock, wants exclusive; P2 has shared lock, wants exclusive upgrade
Both wait for each other's shared lock to release - circular wait
Solution 1: Upgrade should not wait - return error if cannot upgrade immediately
Solution 2: Release all locks before acquiring new lock (non-atomic but safer)
Solution 3: Use lock conversion primitives that handle this atomically
Prevent Hold-and-Wait: Acquire exclusive lock first before releasing shared
Prevent Circular Wait: Define lock acquisition order (total ordering)

questions.question: Design a solution for the readers-writers problem with writer priority using semaphores. Explain how your solution prevents both reader starvation and writer starvation.
keyPoints: Use: mutex (for readcount), rw_mutex (for writers), turnstile/queue for fairness
Variables: readcount (number of readers), mutex (protects readcount)
Writer priority: when writer waiting, new readers blocked at entry
Implementation: check if writer waiting before allowing new readers
Prevent writer starvation: queue ensures fair ordering
Prevent reader starvation (under no-writer scenario): allow concurrent readers
Uses semaphores: binary for mutual exclusion, counting for resource access

questions.question: Explain the different file sharing semantics used in operating systems. Compare UNIX semantics, session semantics, and immutable semantics with respect to consistency, performance, and use cases.
keyPoints: UNIX semantics: immediate visibility - writes visible to all processes with file open
Session semantics: changes visible to other users only after file close-reopen
Immutable semantics: file cannot be modified after creation - maximum consistency
Performance: UNIX most expensive (synchronization), immutable least (no locking)
Use cases: UNIX for local files, session for distributed workstations, immutable for configuration files
Trade-offs: consistency vs performance vs complexity
Considerations: caching, network delays, failure recovery
